aliases: Spaghetti with Pine Nut Pesto and Stracciatella

english: Spaghetti with Pine Nut Pesto and Stracciatella

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for spaghetti pesto. FOODON_00002473 (pasta dish) is the closest defensible parent class. The almond pesto and stracciatella variant is not represented in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked pasta as a category. No specific code exists for spaghetti with pesto sauce in the FoodEx2 hierarchy as of 2024. Closest parent category used as defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for spaghetti pesto dish. SNOMED CT does not enumerate prepared pasta dishes at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of spaghetti pesto is approximately 300 g (one plate), providing around 153 kcal, 4.2 g protein, 4.4 g fat, and 24.2 g carbohydrates.

nutrient_source.title: Spaghetti Pesto — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Spaghetti Pesto (cr_id G00010589). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. The dish description specifies almond pesto, pounded pine nuts, and stracciatella as key components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database — Spaghetti, white, boiled
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spaghetti pesto with almond pesto and stracciatella. GI of 45 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white spaghetti, boiled (GI range 38–61, central estimate ~45). The addition of olive oil, nuts, and cheese in pesto further slows gastric emptying, supporting a GI at or below the lower end of the spaghetti range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 24.15) / 100 ≈ 10.9 for a 300 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 419 mg/100 g; dietary fibre 1.8 g/100 g (below 3 g/100 g rescue threshold); Parmesan cheese in pesto adds additional sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake, request reduced Parmesan or lighter pesto application, and note that the potassium content (319 mg/100 g) provides only partial offset against the elevated sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 8.05 g/100 g; estimated GI ~45 (low); fat and protein from pesto slow gastric emptying; sugar 5.5 g/100 g warrants portion awareness
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can include spaghetti pesto in moderate portions given its low estimated GI (~45) and the glucose-moderating effect of olive oil and nut fat in pesto, but should control serving size to limit total carbohydrate load and request wholemeal pasta to further reduce glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 419 mg/100 g is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low (~0.4 g/100 g); olive oil and pine nuts provide cardioprotective MUFAs and vitamin E
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the elevated sodium content (419 mg/100 g) which can raise blood pressure; the low saturated fat and presence of olive oil and pine nuts are positive factors, but portion control and reduced-sodium preparation are recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Extra-virgin olive oil (oleocanthal, MUFAs), pine nuts (vitamin E), and basil (polyphenols) provide anti-inflammatory benefit; saturated fat is very low (~0.4 g/100 g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Spaghetti pesto is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory ingredient profile, but sodium at 419 mg/100 g may contribute to joint fluid retention and lighter pesto application is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spaghetti Pesto is a Western Italian-style pasta dish served in Singapore, consisting of spaghetti dressed with almond pesto, pounded pine nuts, and stracciatella cheese. The pesto sauce is typically made from basil, extra-virgin olive oil, garlic, Parmesan cheese, and nuts — in this variant, almonds replace or supplement traditional pine nuts, and stracciatella (a fresh pulled-curd cheese) adds a creamy, milky richness. The dish has a mild, herbaceous, nutty flavour with a smooth, slightly oily texture from the pesto and a delicate creaminess from the stracciatella. Per 100 g, it provides 51 kcal, 1.41 g protein, 1.48 g fat, and 8.05 g carbohydrates, with sodium at 419 mg — the primary clinical concern. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 45 (low), moderated by the fat and protein content of the pesto. Dietary fibre is 1.8 g/100 g. The dish is vegetarian and contains gluten, tree nuts (almonds, pine nuts), and dairy. It is suitable for moderate consumption by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes given its low GI, but sodium content makes it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The olive oil and pine nut components contribute cardioprotective monounsaturated fats and anti-inflammatory compounds including oleocanthal and vitamin E.
common_user_queries: How many calories in spaghetti pesto?
Is pesto pasta good for diabetics?
Can I eat spaghetti pesto if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of pesto pasta?
What nuts are in almond pesto?
How much sodium is in spaghetti pesto?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010589) with confidence 0.95. The HPB record is a canonical primary source for Singapore food data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white spaghetti (boiled), which is the closest approved GI source match; this reduces GI confidence to medium but does not affect overall record confidence given the strong macro provenance.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific spaghetti pesto preparation is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in the ingredient profile (olive oil, nuts, fresh cheese); this is a confirmed trace/negligible value consistent with the ingredient composition. saturated_fat_g of 0.4 g/100 g is estimated from the ingredient profile (Parmesan, stracciatella, olive oil) as the HPB record does not separately report saturated fat; this is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for comparable pesto pasta dishes. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish as of 2026-04-16, and no approved secondary source provides a sufficiently close match to support reliable estimation. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white spaghetti boiled; the pesto fat and protein content supports a GI at or below this estimate. GL per serving calculated for a 300 g plate: (45 × 24.15 g carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 10.9.

Both values are now within the allowed set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the validator. This resolves the g5_ontology_honesty hard gate failure.
